Lend me your USB! I need to play Warcraft

For a while we still turned in homework on CDs, then some switched to email. But I forget exactly when in middle / high school, USB drives gradually replaced CDs as the main storage tool. In my memory, USB capacities were 4GB–16GB, priced around NT$300–500.
Later the school added a new rule — all homework must be submitted via USB from now on. I had to walk to a nearby bookshop and buy an 8GB USB.
"Ray, you got a USB?" my classmate asked during computer class.
"Yeah, why?" Whenever a classmate asked if I had something, it usually meant trouble.
"I have a file that's kinda big. Can I borrow your USB to grab a file — we can play together later." He pointed at the screen, and I saw a compressed "GAME" folder. I knew where this was headed.
"OK." I plugged the USB in, ready to copy.
The game was "Warcraft", and every room (host) ran a map called "Nobunaga no Yabou" — version 12.1c, if memory serves.
After that, our computer class went from CS (shooter) to Warcraft.
During the copy, the computer popped a warning:
"File too large to fit in the destination file system."
"After asking my classmate, I learned: to store a single file over 4GB on a USB, the USB had to be formatted to a specific file system first."

Back then I had no idea why that Game folder was so big or where it came from. I learned later it was an "inheritance" left by older students — packed with various game installers, cracks, and even pirated software. No wonder the computer kept warning during the copy.
I eventually got past the too-large-file issue, but a later USB session revealed my computer was infected with a virus — that's another story.
When you get a new memory card or USB, you typically need to do one thing first: format it.
Most of our impression of "format" is:
"Right-click USB, pick Format, a menu pops up, choose a file system, click OK — done."
Did you know there are actually two levels?
There's Low-Level Formatting (LLF) and High-Level Formatting (HLF).
LLF is also called physical formatting. The name doesn't mean grab a hammer and bash your hard drive...
Early hard drives had the disc and mechanical structure assembled at the factory, but the tracks and sectors (logical structure) were blank. Users had to manually do LLF to set them up.
To do this, you'd use BIOS tools or DOS-based dedicated tools to manually partition the disc into a grid and set track and sector positions.

Today, manufacturers do this for you at the factory, so you don't need to. That's why you can plug in a new drive and use it immediately. Only rare cases need attention — BIOS not supporting it, SATA version incompatibility — but we'll skip those details.

Over time, "low-level format" has shifted meaning. The original meaning was partitioning the disc into tracks and sectors. Modern usage usually means erasing all data on the disc and writing zeros to all content — also called "
Every storage unit on the disc gets wiped to 0 — overwriting original data with blanks. In practice, this is more commonly called "disk erasure" or "secure erase", not strictly low-level format.
HLF is also called logical formatting. Every disk has a corresponding file system, and when you format, you're choosing which file system to use, e.g., FAT32, NTFS, exFAT, etc.
Once you pick a file system, the system creates the corresponding file management structure on the disk, e.g.:
FAT32 or exFAT: builds the File Allocation Table (FAT).
NTFS: builds the Master File Table (MFT).
These tables record file names, sizes, and locations, allowing the system to correctly access and manage data.
"Wait, I've never seen these tables before?"
Right — these are written into the disk directly and managed by the system behind the scenes. You don't notice them.
When formatting an entire disk, the system usually also updates or rebuilds the MBR (Master Boot Record) at the start of the disk. MBR records the disk's partition structure and boot info.
So HLF has two key things:
Both are auto-created during formatting. After formatting completes, you can store files on the disk.
Manages file name, size, location info
Records the disk's partition info and boot settings
Now let's talk about file systems.
Common file systems: FAT32, NTFS, exFAT
When talking file systems, the most common are FAT32, NTFS, exFAT.
Before going further, let me briefly intro FAT32 — it has deep historical roots.
Early common file systems included FAT12, FAT16, FAT32 — the numbers refer to bit count, which determines max supported capacity. FAT32 supports max single-file 4GB and max disk 2TB.
So FAT has the best compatibility. For old cameras or old computers, FAT32 is the best choice.

NTFS and exFAT are newer. Both work on Windows and Mac, but NTFS on Mac is read-only — you can't write to it directly. This is because NTFS is Microsoft's own file system, designed primarily for Windows.
exFAT supports both Windows and Mac, with both read and write — great for large files like 4K video or games. It's basically the modern mainstream standard.
Summary table for picking a file system:
Large files, both Windows and Mac
Next time you format a USB, you'll know how to pick the file system~
Basically I lose USB drives nearly every day — like keys, magical disappearance act. They always show up somewhere later.
